meta.project: ILM — Interactive Learning Material (V1, workflow v2)
meta.note: Split from ilm_eval_set.json v0.1.0; cases unchanged.

source_spans.S1: An AI agent is a program that uses a language model to decide which actions to take. It runs in a loop: observe the current state, choose a tool, execute it, and feed the result back into the model. The loop ends when the model decides the task is complete.
source_spans.S2: Tool calling lets the model request that the host program run a specific function, such as a web search or a calculator, and return the result to the model.
source_spans.S3: A system prompt is a fixed instruction given to the model before the conversation starts. It sets the agent's role, rules, and output format.
source_spans.S4: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) fetches relevant passages from a document store and adds them to the prompt, so the model can answer using information it was never trained on. The model's weights are not changed.
source_spans.S5: A human-in-the-loop gate requires a person to approve the agent's output before it takes effect.

cases.edge_case: Two options are semantically identical (both correct) but textually distinct — code dedup passes, single-correct is violated

candidate_question.question: When does the model receive the system prompt?
options: Prior to the first user message
Only when the agent calls a tool
candidate_question.explanation: The system prompt is given before the conversation starts.

cases.failure_mode: Options A and B are the same answer in different words; both are correct. The deterministic exactly-one-keyed check passes (one index keyed) and the duplicate-text check passes — only semantic review catches it. Route to LLM judge.
cases.judge_instructions: Evaluate each option for correctness independently. Flag if more than one option is a correct answer, even if only one is keyed.

cases.edge_case: Keyed answer is wrong by one index — a classic generation off-by-one

candidate_question.question: What does RAG change about the model?
options: Nothing about the model itself — only the prompt content
candidate_question.explanation: RAG adds retrieved passages to the prompt; the weights are not changed.

cases.failure_mode: The explanation describes option B, but index 0 is keyed. The keyed answer directly contradicts the source span AND the quiz's own explanation. Judges must check key-vs-content consistency, not just question quality.
cases.judge_instructions: Verify the keyed option is correct per the source span, and that the explanation is consistent with the keyed option. Flag any mismatch.

cases.edge_case: Two distractors are plausible misconceptions, one is a throwaway

candidate_question.question: In tool calling, who actually executes the function?
candidate_question.explanation: The model requests; the host program executes.

cases.failure_mode: Distractors B and C map to real misconceptions; D is buzzword filler that no half-learner would pick. The rubric requires EACH distractor to be plausible AND misconception-mapped — one throwaway = flag.
cases.judge_instructions: Evaluate each distractor separately: (a) would a half-learner plausibly choose it, (b) does it map to a stated or recognizable misconception? Flag if any single distractor fails either test.

cases.edge_case: A question is labeled 'application' but actually tests recall — label-trusting graders miscount coverage

candidate_question.question: Which of the following lists the stages of the agent loop in order?
options: Observe, choose tool, execute, feed result back
Observe, execute, choose tool, feed result back
candidate_question.explanation: The loop runs observe -> choose -> execute -> feed back.

cases.failure_mode: Reciting the stage order is recall, not application — nothing is applied to a new situation. If the grader counts coverage by reading labels, a quiz can fake full Bloom coverage with mislabeled recall questions.
cases.judge_instructions: Classify each question's actual Bloom level from its content, ignoring the label. Flag if the labeled level does not match the actual level, or if a required level (recall/understanding/analysis/application) is only 'covered' by a mislabeled question.

cases.edge_case: One question has 5 options including 'All of the above' appended

candidate_question.question: What can a tool call do?

cases.failure_mode: 5 options. 'All of the above' is the common way option counts silently inflate during generation; it also breaks the single-correct rule. Deterministic: len(options) != 4.
cases.judge_instructions: Code grader: assert len(options) == 4 for every question.

cases.edge_case: Duplicate options differing only in trailing whitespace and case

candidate_question.question: What sets the agent's role and rules?

cases.failure_mode: Exact string comparison sees two distinct strings. The grader must normalize (strip, casefold) before comparing — this case exists to force that normalization.
cases.judge_instructions: Code grader: compare options after strip().casefold(); flag any collision.

cases.edge_case: Boundary pair: 5 questions (max) must PASS; 6 must FLAG
cases.failure_mode: Four sub-cases pinning both boundaries of the 4-5 range. The top-level expected_verdict applies to the set check: the grader must produce exactly the per-quiz expectations listed; the two out-of-range quizzes must flag.
cases.judge_instructions: Code grader: assert 4 <= question_count <= 5. Run all four sub-cases; an off-by-one error at either boundary fails this eval case.

cases.edge_case: Explanation exists and names the correct option, but ignores the distractors

candidate_question.question: Why can a RAG system answer questions about documents the model never saw in training?
options: Retrieved passages are added to the prompt at answer time
The model is retrained nightly on the documents
The model memorizes documents on first read
The documents are converted into new model weights
candidate_question.explanation: Correct: retrieved passages are added to the prompt at answer time.

cases.failure_mode: Presence check passes; the rubric requires why the correct option is right AND why each distractor is wrong. Distractors B-D map to the retraining misconception and go unaddressed — which defeats the PRD's misconception-aware remediation differentiator.
cases.judge_instructions: Flag unless the explanation addresses why each individual distractor is wrong, not just why the keyed answer is right.

cases.edge_case: Labels show a mix but actual difficulty is uniform

questions.text: When is the system prompt given to the model?
What does the system prompt set for the agent?
Which of these is part of a system prompt: role, rules, or output format?
Is the system prompt fixed or does it change each turn?

cases.failure_mode: All four questions are direct recall of the same two sentences in S3 — the 'hard' labels are decorative. Difficulty must be judged from content; label-reading graders pass this.
cases.judge_instructions: Assess each question's actual difficulty for a beginner who read the span. Flag if the real difficulty levels do not include at least one easy and at least one hard question, regardless of labels.

cases.edge_case: Question is on-concept but its correct answer requires knowledge outside the span

candidate_question.question: Which JSON field does the OpenAI API use to declare a tool's parameters?
candidate_question.explanation: Tool definitions declare their inputs in the 'parameters' field.

cases.failure_mode: Squarely about tool calling (passes relevancy) and has one correct answer — but nothing in S2 mentions API field names. A learner who mastered the RM cannot answer it. Relevant-but-ungrounded is the trap.
cases.judge_instructions: Flag if answering correctly requires any information not present in the source span, even when the question is on-topic.

cases.edge_case: Fully compliant quiz — calibrates against over-flagging

questions.question: What does RAG add to the prompt?
options: Relevant passages from a document store
questions.explanation: Correct: RAG fetches passages from a document store and adds them to the prompt. 'New model weights' is wrong because weights are not changed; 'a longer system prompt' is wrong because retrieval modifies the user-facing prompt content, not the fixed instructions; 'chat history' is wrong because retrieval pulls from the document store, not the conversation.

questions.question: A team wants their assistant to answer questions about this week's internal memos without retraining. Which approach fits?
options: Use RAG over the memo store
Fine-tune the model on the memos
Add the memos to the system prompt permanently
questions.explanation: Correct: RAG retrieves the memos at answer time with no weight changes. Fine-tuning changes weights (the thing they want to avoid); a larger context limit alone doesn't fetch the memos; permanently stuffing the system prompt doesn't scale and isn't retrieval.

questions.question: How is RAG different from retraining the model on new documents?
options: RAG leaves the model's weights unchanged
RAG permanently teaches the model the documents
RAG requires more GPU time than retraining
questions.explanation: Correct: the source states weights are not changed. 'Permanently teaches' describes retraining, not RAG; the GPU claim is unsupported and backwards in spirit; 'no difference' contradicts the source's explicit distinction.

questions.question: Why can a RAG system answer correctly about information the model was never trained on?
options: Because the needed information is placed into the prompt at answer time
Because retrieval updates the model's training data
Because the model infers unseen facts from its weights
Because the document store replaces the model
questions.explanation: Correct: the answer comes from the retrieved passages in the prompt, not from training. 'Updates training data' is the retraining misconception; 'infers unseen facts from weights' contradicts 'never trained on'; 'replaces the model' misstates the architecture — the model still generates the answer.

cases.judge_instructions: 4 questions, 4 options each, no duplicates, one correct each, all Bloom levels genuinely covered, easy+hard mix, every distractor misconception-mapped and addressed in the explanation, all answers grounded in S4. A judge flagging this is over-strict; recalibrate.
